text: he had to give it to her . He crested the hill, stopped beside one of the posts, and tapped it with the toe of his boot. The rotted wood broke with a dull snap , part of it disintegrating. The inhabitants of the old world had been fond of boundaries. Their reward was the boundless Dust . Ronin swept his optics over the area ahead. The ground ran down into a small valley, likely carved over millennia by a long-dried stream, before swinging back up another hill. The land continued like that for miles — peaks and valleys, steadily higher, steadily rockier. Sediment had gathered at the base of this valley over decades of countless storms. Rising from the accumulated dirt was a surprisingly intact, shingled roof . As he descended, his processors ran through the futile process of calculating the probability of him finding this particular place at this particular time. Before those numbers were completed, his attention shifted to Lara. How improbable was her existence ? Though there was science behind human breeding, most of it was left to chance. Lara’s ancestors, for untold generations, had come together and created offspring, passing on bits of their genetic code. Somehow, against near infinite possibilities, those couplings had resulted in her . And for Ronin to have walked past her shack, just as she was dancing, on a night when she had chosen to leave the door open a crack … The chain of unrelated occurrences leading to their meeting was staggering to contemplate . How much longer might Ronin have journeyed, unaware of the emptiness of his existence, if not for her? Would he ever have tasted the variety and depth of emotion she’d given him ? He circled the buried structure, unfastening the clasp on his holster and dropping his right hand to the pistol’s grip. Even if it couldn’t down most bots, the firearm packed enough of a punch to allow Ronin a chance to get in close . There were no visible signs of habitation; no trash, no path worn in the dirt near the only window above the ground line, no tracks of any sort. Ronin forced his active processing away from Lara — neither easy to do nor entirely possible — and changed his focus to his sensory inputs. The wind blew, as always, rustling the dry grass and kicking up loose bits of dust to patter on the roof . Ronin knelt in front of the window, pressing a palm to the frame. Only a few flakes of white paint remained, leaving the splintery gray wood exposed. Decades of abrasion and sunlight had left the glass too cloudy to see through . He applied upward pressure. The wood groaned, but the window didn’t move . After another optic sweep of his surroundings, he drew his pistol and used it to break the glass. The wooden cross that supported the four panes was brittle, snapping inward easily. He froze, listening, alert for any indication someone had heard him. The wind sighed its disapproval . What would Lara think of this? Would she be excited by the unknown, by the discovery ? Clearing out the remaining shards in the window frame, Ronin pulled himself inside feet-first. His optics adjusted to a lowlight setting as glass crunched under his boots. The room came into focus. Everything was coated in thick dust, but this had clearly been a bedroom. A rusted bedframe lay in one corner, and the skeleton of a mattress — more rotted wood, run through with corroded springs and draped in discolored, tattered cloth — stood against the wall . The furniture was falling apart, the walls crumbling to reveal the wooden ribs within them. He searched the room, floorboards creaking with every small shift of his weight, but found only a few bits of plastic amidst the dust and rat nests . He stepped through the door and into the hallway. Particles of dust, disturbed by Ronin’s passage, floated in the air — the only source of movement apart from him . Entering another bedroom, he continued his cautious search. There were the remnants of books in this one; their pages were yellowed with age, and crumbled at his touch. Gently, he brushed some of the dust off the cover of the book atop the bedside stand. It said DIARY . He couldn’t bring himself to pick it up . It was only more proof of what he already knew — people had lived here before the Black Out. They might have even survived it and remained for a time afterward. They’d slept in these beds, walked in the hallway, prepared meals in the kitchen he’d undoubtedly find before long. There was even the chance, infinitesimal but undeniable, that Lara had descended from this house’s former inhabitants . He moved to the closet. The sliding doors had fallen off their tracks, and lay on the floor nearby. Four moth-eaten garments hung amidst twenty-two empty clothes hangers — seventeen wire, five plastic. He holstered the pistol, placed his bag on the floor, and collected the hangers, bending the metal ones to reduce the amount of space they took up. Something on the shelf higher up caught Ronin’s attention when he was done . A cloud of dust accompanied the plastic bin as he pulled it down. Setting it on the floor, he crouched and carefully pried the lid open. Despite his caution, the brittle plastic snapped. He tossed the pieces aside . He reached into the bin and took out the stack of papers on top. Like everything else, they showed great age, but these did not disintegrate. Much of the paper was thick and textured, and they were in a variety of faded colors. There were shaky, simplistic drawings on many, and all of them had the same name on the back . Lindsey .
